title: Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume
text: glmassume package provides visual statistical diagnostics generalized linear models (GLMs). supports common GLM families: binomial, Poisson, negative binomial, Gamma, inverse Gaussian, Gaussian. vignette demonstrates diagnostics three families: Binomial (logistic regression) Poisson (count data) Gaussian (linear regression via GLM) diagnostics implemented follow frameworks presented : Hosmer, D.W., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R.X. (2013). Applied Logistic Regression, 3rd Edition. John Wiley & Sons. Chapters 4–5. Kleiber, C. Zeileis, . (2016). Visualizing Count Data Regressions Using Rootograms. American Statistician, 70(3), 296–303.

title: Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume
text: ’ll use mtcars dataset model probability car manual transmission (= 1) based miles per gallon (mpg), weight (wt), horsepower (hp).
code: model_binom <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = binomial) summary(model_binom) #>  #> Call: #> glm(formula = am ~ mpg + wt + hp, family = binomial, data = mtcars) #>  #> Coefficients: #>              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   #> (Intercept) -15.72137   40.00281  -0.393   0.6943   #> mpg           1.22930    1.58109   0.778   0.4369   #> wt           -6.95492    3.35297  -2.074   0.0381 * #> hp            0.08389    0.08228   1.020   0.3079   #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1) #>  #>     Null deviance: 43.2297  on 31  degrees of freedom #> Residual deviance:  8.7661  on 28  degrees of freedom #> AIC: 16.766 #>  #> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 10

title: Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume
text: key assumption logistic regression continuous predictor linear relationship log-odds (logit) outcome. plot_linearity_link() function overlays loess smoother (blue) linear fit (red dashed) predictor vs. predicted link value.   look : blue loess curve track closely red dashed line. Curvature systematic departures suggest may need transformation (e.g., polynomial spline term) predictor.

title: Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume
text: Hosmer-Lemeshow test specifically designed binomial models.

title: Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume
text: count data, use Poisson model. key additional concerns overdispersion zero inflation.
code: counts <- c(18, 17, 15, 20, 10, 20, 25, 13, 12) outcome <- gl(3, 1, 9) treatment <- gl(3, 3) model_pois <- glm(counts ~ outcome + treatment, family = poisson) summary(model_pois) #>  #> Call: #> glm(formula = counts ~ outcome + treatment, family = poisson) #>  #> Coefficients: #>               Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)     #> (Intercept)  3.045e+00  1.709e-01  17.815   <2e-16 *** #> outcome2    -4.543e-01  2.022e-01  -2.247   0.0246 *   #> outcome3    -2.930e-01  1.927e-01  -1.520   0.1285     #> treatment2   1.217e-15  2.000e-01   0.000   1.0000     #> treatment3   8.438e-16  2.000e-01   0.000   1.0000     #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> (Dispersion parameter for poisson family taken to be 1) #>  #>     Null deviance: 10.5814  on 8  degrees of freedom #> Residual deviance:  5.1291  on 4  degrees of freedom #> AIC: 56.761 #>  #> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

title: Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume
code: overdispersion_test(model_pois) #> Overdispersion Test (Pearson Chi-Squared) #> ------------------------------------------ #> Family: poisson #> Dispersion estimate: 1.2933 #> Pearson Chi-sq: 5.1732  df: 4  p-value: 0.2700 #> => Evidence of overdispersion (dispersion > 1) #>    Consider: quasipoisson, negative binomial, or robust standard errors

title: Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume
code: dispersion_test(model_pois) #> Dispersion Diagnostic #> --------------------- #> Family: poisson #> Estimated dispersion: 1.2933 #> Residual df: 4 #> Pearson Chi-sq: 5.1732  p-value: 0.2700 #> => Dispersion > 1 (potential overdispersion) #>    Consider: quasi-family, negative binomial, or robust standard errors

title: Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume
text: rootogram compares observed expected frequencies square-root scale. “hanging” rootogram, bars dip axis indicate model -predicts counts.

title: Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume
text: diagnostics work Gaussian (identity link) models.
code: model_gauss <- glm(mpg ~ wt + hp + disp, data = mtcars, family = gaussian) summary(model_gauss) #>  #> Call: #> glm(formula = mpg ~ wt + hp + disp, family = gaussian, data = mtcars) #>  #> Coefficients: #>              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)     #> (Intercept) 37.105505   2.110815  17.579  < 2e-16 *** #> wt          -3.800891   1.066191  -3.565  0.00133 **  #> hp          -0.031157   0.011436  -2.724  0.01097 *   #> disp        -0.000937   0.010350  -0.091  0.92851     #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> (Dispersion parameter for gaussian family taken to be 6.963953) #>  #>     Null deviance: 1126.05  on 31  degrees of freedom #> Residual deviance:  194.99  on 28  degrees of freedom #> AIC: 158.64 #>  #> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 2

title: Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume
text: Gaussian GLM identity link, checks predictors linear relationship response.

title: Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume
text: Gaussian models, dispersion estimated (fixed), hypothesis test performed.
code: dispersion_test(model_gauss) #> Dispersion Diagnostic #> --------------------- #> Family: gaussian #> Estimated dispersion: 6.9640 #> Residual df: 28 #> (Dispersion is estimated, not fixed; no hypothesis test performed) #>    Review residual plots to assess whether the estimate is stable

title: Diagnosing GLMs with glmassume
text: Belsley, D.., Kuh, E., Welsch, R.E. (1980). Regression Diagnostics: Identifying Influential Data Sources Collinearity. John Wiley & Sons. Box, G.E.P. Tidwell, P.W. (1962). Transformation independent variables. Technometrics, 4(4), 531–550. Cook, R.D. (1977). Detection influential observation linear regression. Technometrics, 19(1), 15–18. Hoaglin, D.C. Welsch, R.E. (1978). hat matrix regression ANOVA. American Statistician, 32(1), 17–22. Hosmer, D.W. Lemeshow, S. (1980). Goodness fit tests multiple logistic regression model. Communications Statistics – Theory Methods, 9(10), 1043–1069. Hosmer, D.W. Sturdivant, R.X. (2007). smoothed residual based goodness--fit statistic logistic hierarchical regression models. Computational Statistics & Data Analysis, 51(8), 3898–3912. Hosmer, D.W., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R.X. (2013). Applied Logistic Regression, 3rd Edition. John Wiley & Sons. Kleiber, C. Zeileis, . (2016). Visualizing Count Data Regressions Using Rootograms. American Statistician, 70(3), 296–303. Marquardt, D.W. (1970). Generalized inverses, ridge regression, biased linear estimation, nonlinear estimation. Technometrics, 12(3), 591–612. McCullagh, P. Nelder, J.. (1989). Generalized Linear Models, 2nd Edition. Chapman & Hall. Ozkale, M.R., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R. (2018). Logistic regression diagnostics ridge regression. Computational Statistics, 33, 563–593. Paul, P., Pennell, M.L., Lemeshow, S. (2013). Standardizing power Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness fit test large data sets. Statistics Medicine, 32(1), 67–80. Pregibon, D. (1981). Logistic regression diagnostics. Annals Statistics, 9(4), 705–724.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Dusty Turner. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Turner D (2026). glmassume: Visualize Diagnose GLM Assumptions. R package version 0.1.0, https://dustysturner.com/glmassume/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {glmassume: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions},   author = {Dusty Turner},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://dustysturner.com/glmassume/}, }

title: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions
text: glmassume provides visual statistical diagnostics checking assumptions generalized linear models fitted glm(). Supports binomial, Poisson, negative binomial, Gamma, inverse Gaussian, Gaussian families. Supply fitted model get publication-ready diagnostic plots built ggplot2.

title: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions
text: Fit model run diagnostics one call. diagnose_glm() automatically selects family-appropriate diagnostics.

title: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions
code: library(glmassume)  model_binom <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = binomial) diagnose_glm(model_binom)

title: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions
code: counts <- c(18, 17, 15, 20, 10, 20, 25, 13, 12) outcome <- gl(3, 1, 9) treatment <- gl(3, 3) model_pois <- glm(counts ~ outcome + treatment, family = poisson) diagnose_glm(model_pois)

title: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions
text: continuous predictor linear relationship linear predictor (log-odds binomial, log Poisson, etc.). blue loess curve track red dashed linear fit.

title: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions
text: loess smoother roughly flat centered near zero.

title: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions
text: Points dashed threshold (4/n4/n) warrant investigation.  DFBETAS show observation affects individual coefficient estimates.

title: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions
text: goodness--fit test Hosmer, Lemeshow, & Sturdivant (2013). non-significant p-value suggests adequate fit.

title: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions
code: overdispersion_test(model_pois) #> Overdispersion Test (Pearson Chi-Squared) #> ------------------------------------------ #> Family: poisson #> Dispersion estimate: 1.2933 #> Pearson Chi-sq: 5.1732  df: 4  p-value: 0.2700 #> => Evidence of overdispersion (dispersion > 1)

title: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions
text: VIF values 5 suggest moderate multicollinearity; 10 severe.

title: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions
text: Hosmer, D.W., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R.X. (2013). Applied Logistic Regression, 3rd Edition. John Wiley & Sons. Kleiber, C. Zeileis, . (2016). Visualizing Count Data Regressions Using Rootograms. American Statistician, 70(3), 296–303.

title: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions
text: Browse walkthrough vignette guided tutorial File bugs feature requests https://github.com/dusty-turner/glmassume/issues

title: Diagnose GLM Assumptions — diagnose_glm
text: Produces panel diagnostic plots assessing assumptions generalized linear model. Automatically detects model family selects appropriate diagnostics.

title: Diagnose GLM Assumptions — diagnose_glm
text: model fitted glm object. guide Logical. Show interpretive subtitles plots? Defaults getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE).

title: Diagnose GLM Assumptions — diagnose_glm
text: combined plot (requires patchwork package layout). patchwork installed, returns list individual ggplot objects.

title: Diagnose GLM Assumptions — diagnose_glm
text: diagnostics selected depend model family: families receive residual Cook's distance plots. Binomial: adds Hosmer-Lemeshow calibration plot linearity--link. Poisson / Negative Binomial: adds rootogram zero-inflation diagnostic. Gamma / Inverse Gaussian / Gaussian: adds linearity--link plots.

title: Diagnose GLM Assumptions — diagnose_glm
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial) diagnose_glm(m) #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'   m2 <- glm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = gaussian) diagnose_glm(m2) #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Dispersion Test — dispersion_test
text: Estimates tests dispersion parameter fitted GLM. families dispersion fixed 1 (binomial, Poisson), tests whether true dispersion differs 1. families estimated dispersion (Gamma, inverse Gaussian, Gaussian), reports estimated value.

title: Dispersion Test — dispersion_test
text: model fitted glm object.

title: Dispersion Test — dispersion_test
text: list class "dispersion_test" containing: dispersion Estimated dispersion parameter. family model family name. fixed_dispersion Logical; TRUE family assumes dispersion = 1. statistic Pearson chi-squared statistic (fixed-dispersion families). df Residual degrees freedom. p_value P-value testing dispersion = 1 (fixed-dispersion families; NA otherwise).

title: Dispersion Test — dispersion_test
code: # Poisson model — tests whether dispersion = 1 counts <- c(18, 17, 15, 20, 10, 20, 25, 13, 12) outcome <- gl(3, 1, 9) treatment <- gl(3, 3) m <- glm(counts ~ outcome + treatment, family = poisson) dispersion_test(m) #> Dispersion Diagnostic #> --------------------- #> Family: poisson #> Estimated dispersion: 1.2933 #> Residual df: 4 #> Pearson Chi-sq: 5.1732  p-value: 0.2700 #> => Dispersion > 1 (potential overdispersion) #>    Consider: quasi-family, negative binomial, or robust standard errors  # Gamma model — reports estimated dispersion m2 <- glm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log")) dispersion_test(m2) #> Dispersion Diagnostic #> --------------------- #> Family: Gamma #> Estimated dispersion: 0.0133 #> Residual df: 29 #> (Dispersion is estimated, not fixed; no hypothesis test performed) #>    Review residual plots to assess whether the estimate is stable

what: glmassume: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions — glmassume-package
title: glmassume: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions — glmassume-package
text: teaching-focused toolkit visualizing diagnosing assumptions generalized linear models (GLMs). Every plot includes interpretive subtitle every test suggests next steps, making ideal students, instructors, analysts learning GLMs. Set guide = FALSE (options(glmassume.guide = FALSE)) publication-ready output. Supports binomial, Poisson, negative binomial, Gamma, inverse Gaussian, Gaussian families.

title: glmassume: Visualize and Diagnose GLM Assumptions — glmassume-package
text: Maintainer: Dusty Turner dusty.turner@westpoint.edu

title: Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit Test — hoslem_test
text: Performs Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness--fit test fitted logistic regression model. Observations sorted fitted probabilities, divided g groups, chi-squared statistic computed comparing observed expected event counts within group.

title: Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit Test — hoslem_test
text: model fitted glm object family = binomial. g Integer. Number groups (default 10).

title: Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit Test — hoslem_test
text: list class "hoslem_test" containing: statistic Hosmer-Lemeshow chi-squared test statistic. df Degrees freedom (g - 2). p_value P-value chi-squared distribution. table data frame observed expected counts per group. g Number groups used.

title: Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit Test — hoslem_test
text: Hosmer, D.W., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R.X. (2013). Applied Logistic Regression, 3rd Edition. John Wiley & Sons.

title: Overdispersion Test — overdispersion_test
text: Tests overdispersion GLM comparing sum squared Pearson residuals expected value assumed model. useful Poisson negative binomial models, can applied GLM dispersion parameter assumed 1.

title: Overdispersion Test — overdispersion_test
text: model fitted glm object.

title: Overdispersion Test — overdispersion_test
text: list class "overdispersion_test" containing: dispersion Estimated dispersion (sum squared Pearson residuals divided residual degrees freedom). statistic Pearson chi-squared statistic. df Residual degrees freedom. p_value P-value chi-squared distribution (upper tail). family model family name.

title: Overdispersion Test — overdispersion_test
text: test computes sum(pearson_resid^2) compares chi-squared distribution n - p degrees freedom. dispersion estimate substantially greater 1 suggests overdispersion. significant p-value indicates variance larger expected assumed distribution.

title: Overdispersion Test — overdispersion_test
code: counts <- c(18, 17, 15, 20, 10, 20, 25, 13, 12) outcome <- gl(3, 1, 9) treatment <- gl(3, 3) m <- glm(counts ~ outcome + treatment, family = poisson) overdispersion_test(m) #> Overdispersion Test (Pearson Chi-Squared) #> ------------------------------------------ #> Family: poisson #> Dispersion estimate: 1.2933 #> Pearson Chi-sq: 5.1732  df: 4  p-value: 0.2700 #> => Evidence of overdispersion (dispersion > 1) #>    Consider: quasipoisson, negative binomial, or robust standard errors

title: Plot Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit — plot_hoslem
text: Visualizes results Hosmer-Lemeshow test plotting observed versus expected event proportions across decile groups. Points falling near 45-degree line indicate good calibration.

title: Plot Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit — plot_hoslem
text: model fitted glm object family = binomial. g Integer. Number groups (default 10). guide Logical. Show interpretive subtitle? Defaults getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE).

title: Plot Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit — plot_hoslem
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit — plot_hoslem
text: Hosmer, D.W., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R.X. (2013). Applied Logistic Regression, 3rd Edition. John Wiley & Sons.

title: Plot Hosmer-Lemeshow Goodness-of-Fit — plot_hoslem
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial) plot_hoslem(m)

title: Plot Influential Observations — plot_influence
text: Produces diagnostic plots identifying influential observations fitted GLM. Includes Cook's distance, leverage (hat values), delta-beta (DFBETAS) plots. Works GLM family.

title: Plot Influential Observations — plot_influence
code: plot_influence(   model,   type = c("cooks", "leverage", "dfbetas"),   threshold_multiplier = 1,   guide = getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE) )

title: Plot Influential Observations — plot_influence
text: model fitted glm object. type Character. One "cooks" (default), "leverage", "dfbetas". threshold_multiplier Numeric multiplier rule--thumb cutoff line. Cook's distance, cutoff threshold_multiplier * 4/n. leverage, threshold_multiplier * 2*p/n. guide Logical. Show interpretive subtitle? Defaults getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE).

title: Plot Influential Observations — plot_influence
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot Influential Observations — plot_influence
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial) plot_influence(m, type = "cooks")  plot_influence(m, type = "leverage")   m2 <- glm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = gaussian) plot_influence(m2)

what: Plot Linearity of the Link Function — plot_linearity_link
title: Plot Linearity of the Link Function — plot_linearity_link
text: continuous predictor GLM, plots predictor linear predictor (link scale) using loess smoother. linear relationship suggests linearity assumption link scale satisfied. Works GLM family.

title: Plot Linearity of the Link Function — plot_linearity_link
code: plot_linearity_link(   model,   predictors = NULL,   guide = getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE) )

title: Plot Linearity of the Link Function — plot_linearity_link
text: model fitted glm object. predictors Character vector predictor names plot. Defaults numeric predictors model. guide Logical. Show interpretive subtitle? Defaults getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE).

title: Plot Linearity of the Link Function — plot_linearity_link
text: list ggplot objects, one per predictor.

title: Plot Linearity of the Link Function — plot_linearity_link
text: binomial models checks linearity logit; Poisson models checks linearity log scale; link functions. blue loess curve track closely red dashed linear fit. Deviations suggest transformation (e.g., polynomial spline) may needed.

title: Plot Linearity of the Link Function — plot_linearity_link
text: Hosmer, D.W., Lemeshow, S., Sturdivant, R.X. (2013). Applied Logistic Regression, 3rd Edition. John Wiley & Sons, Chapter 4.

title: Plot Linearity of the Link Function — plot_linearity_link
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial) plots <- plot_linearity_link(m) plots[[1]] #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'   m2 <- glm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = gaussian) plots2 <- plot_linearity_link(m2)

what: Plot Linearity of the Logit (Deprecated) — plot_linearity_logit
title: Plot Linearity of the Logit (Deprecated) — plot_linearity_logit
text: plot_linearity_logit() superseded plot_linearity_link(), works GLM family. function now calls plot_linearity_link() internally.

title: Plot Linearity of the Logit (Deprecated) — plot_linearity_logit
Plot Linearity of the Logit (Deprecated) — plot_linearity_logit
text: model fitted glm object family = binomial. predictors Character vector predictor names plot. Defaults numeric predictors model.

title: Plot Linearity of the Logit (Deprecated) — plot_linearity_logit
text: list ggplot objects, one per predictor.

title: Plot Linearity of the Logit (Deprecated) — plot_linearity_logit
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial) plots <- plot_linearity_logit(m) #> Warning: 'plot_linearity_logit' is deprecated. #> Use 'plot_linearity_link' instead. #> See help("Deprecated") plots[[1]] #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Plot Residual Diagnostics — plot_residuals
text: Produces residual diagnostic plots fitted GLM. Supports deviance residuals, Pearson residuals, response residuals plotted fitted values observation index. Works GLM family.

title: Plot Residual Diagnostics — plot_residuals
code: plot_residuals(   model,   type = c("deviance", "pearson", "response"),   vs = c("fitted", "index"),   guide = getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE) )

title: Plot Residual Diagnostics — plot_residuals
text: model fitted glm object. type Character. Type residual: "deviance" (default), "pearson", "response". vs Character. plot residuals : "fitted" (default) "index". guide Logical. Show interpretive subtitle? Defaults getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE).

title: Plot Residual Diagnostics — plot_residuals
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot Residual Diagnostics — plot_residuals
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial) plot_residuals(m) #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'  plot_residuals(m, type = "pearson", vs = "index") #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'   m2 <- glm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = gaussian) plot_residuals(m2) #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

what: Plot Rootogram for Count Models — plot_rootogram
title: Plot Rootogram for Count Models — plot_rootogram
text: Creates hanging standing rootogram count models (Poisson negative binomial). rootogram compares observed expected frequencies square-root scale, making easier detect departures assumed distribution.

title: Plot Rootogram for Count Models — plot_rootogram
code: plot_rootogram(   model,   style = c("hanging", "standing"),   max_count = NULL,   guide = getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE) )

title: Plot Rootogram for Count Models — plot_rootogram
text: model fitted glm object family = poisson MASS::glm.nb() model. style Character. "hanging" (default) suspends observed bars expected curve; "standing" places observed bars x-axis. max_count Integer. Maximum count display (default: auto-detected data). Counts beyond aggregated. guide Logical. Show interpretive subtitle? Defaults getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE).

title: Plot Rootogram for Count Models — plot_rootogram
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot Rootogram for Count Models — plot_rootogram
text: Poisson model, expected frequencies count \(k\) computed sum(dpois(k, fitted(model))). negative binomial models, sum(dnbinom(k, mu = fitted(model), size = model$theta)). hanging rootogram, bars extend x-axis indicate model -predicts count; bars stop axis indicate -prediction.

title: Plot Rootogram for Count Models — plot_rootogram
text: Kleiber, C. Zeileis, . (2016). Visualizing Count Data Regressions Using Rootograms. American Statistician, 70(3), 296–303.

title: Plot Rootogram for Count Models — plot_rootogram
code: counts <- c(18, 17, 15, 20, 10, 20, 25, 13, 12) outcome <- gl(3, 1, 9) treatment <- gl(3, 3) m <- glm(counts ~ outcome + treatment, family = poisson) plot_rootogram(m)

title: Plot Variance Inflation Factors — plot_vif
text: Computes visualizes variance inflation factors (VIF) predictors fitted GLM. High VIF values (commonly > 5 > 10) indicate multicollinearity. Works GLM family.

title: Plot Variance Inflation Factors — plot_vif
text: model fitted glm object. threshold Numeric. VIF threshold reference line (default 5). guide Logical. Show interpretive subtitle? Defaults getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE).

title: Plot Variance Inflation Factors — plot_vif
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot Variance Inflation Factors — plot_vif
text: VIF computed regressing predictor remaining predictors using OLS calculating 1 / (1 - R^2). models one predictor, VIF meaningful function stop message.

title: Plot Variance Inflation Factors — plot_vif
code: m <- glm(am ~ mpg + wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = binomial) plot_vif(m)   m2 <- glm(mpg ~ wt + hp + disp, data = mtcars, family = gaussian) plot_vif(m2)

title: Plot Zero-Inflation Diagnostic — plot_zero_inflation
text: Compares observed proportion zeros expected proportion fitted count model. bar chart shows observed vs expected zero counts side side, making easy detect zero inflation.

title: Plot Zero-Inflation Diagnostic — plot_zero_inflation
text: model fitted glm object family = poisson MASS::glm.nb() model. guide Logical. Show interpretive subtitle? Defaults getOption("glmassume.guide", TRUE).

title: Plot Zero-Inflation Diagnostic — plot_zero_inflation
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot Zero-Inflation Diagnostic — plot_zero_inflation
text: Poisson models, expected number zeros sum(dpois(0, fitted(model))). negative binomial models, sum(dnbinom(0, mu = fitted(model), size = model$theta)). observed zero count substantially larger expected, data may zero-inflated zero-inflated hurdle model might appropriate.

title: Plot Zero-Inflation Diagnostic — plot_zero_inflation
code: counts <- c(18, 17, 15, 20, 10, 20, 25, 13, 12) outcome <- gl(3, 1, 9) treatment <- gl(3, 3) m <- glm(counts ~ outcome + treatment, family = poisson) plot_zero_inflation(m)
